text: She didn’t give a shit about what they’d say. Ronin was alive, and Lara loved him . The warm sunshine on her face cast a reddish glow through her eyelids. She opened her eyes; the sun was setting, spreading muted pinks and oranges through the haze beyond the park. One day down, two or three more to go. Once he got back, they’d sell all the scrap and go, and she’d never have to worry about Warlord again. She wasn’t so naïve as to think there’d be no other danger, but she couldn’t imagine anything worse than dealing with him again . She picked up the rifle and descended the ladder . By the time she ate and showered, it was full dark outside. Lara double-checked the locks — even the minor security they provided was better than nothing — and went up to Ronin’s room. She slipped beneath the blankets he only used when he laid with her, laid the rifle beside her, and rested her head on his pillow . Lara inhaled deeply; it was faint, but his earthy scent lingered . Nights were the worst when she was waiting for Ronin. After Tabitha left, Lara had spent her nights alone, and slept soundly despite her concerns. Why was it so difficult now? Every noise the house made, every subtle creak and groan, had her hand tightening around the rifle . The answer was simple. Ronin made her feel safe, not this place. She trusted him enough to let her guard down. Even before they shared a bed, she’d been aware of his presence, and knew he’d come at the first sign of trouble. Just like her first night here, when she nearly broke her ass on the cold bathroom floor . The sooner she went to sleep, the sooner tomorrow would come, and she’d be that much closer to his return. Breathing deeply, she relaxed, imaging his body pressed against hers . Just as she was drifting off, a thump startled her awake. She lay there, staring at the ceiling, and strained to hear more. A bang downstairs had her scrambling from beneath the covers. She snatched up the rifle and crept to the wall, chest heaving and heart pounding . Another thump , followed by a scrape, like something was dragged across the floor. Swallowing hard, she adjusted her grip on the gun, holding it how Ronin showed her before he left. She wasn’t going to hide and wait for whatever that was to catch her. Lara would fight. If she was going to die tonight, it wouldn’t be cowering in the dark . She slipped through the open door and into the hallway, walking silently across the soft carpet. Pressing herself to the wall beside the stairs, she took another stabilizing breath, and waited . There was more noise below. A heavy step, and then that dragging sound. It repeated a few times, ending with the groaning wood of the chair at Ronin’s worktable. Whatever the thing was, it was sitting in Ronin’s chair . Lara peered around the corner. It was dark in the main room; she’d kept the curtains drawn while he was away, for fear of being seen. Everything was silent now, except her own heartbeat . Jumping out a second-floor window would mean a broken leg, most likely, which was as good as death, and getting into the attic now would be too noisy. There were other places to hide, but if it started looking…why the hell was it just sitting down there ? Fuck . Squeezing her eyes shut, she leaned her head back against the wall. I can do this. Ronin said this thing packs a punch, even against bots . If she could at least hit the thing, she might have a chance to run out the front door and hide somewhere else until Ronin came back. That meant she’d have to see it. Once she flipped the switch, she’d only have a second to aim and fire. So long as it hadn’t been moved, she knew exactly where the chair was, knew where to point the gun … Every step down the stairs was agonizing, her lungs burning with the exertion of keeping her breath silent. The carpet padded her footfalls, but she settled her weight carefully, knowing the softest creak would give her away . She focused her eyes on the shadows where the chair would be, darting her gaze to the side only to spot the barely-visible white of the light switch on the nearby wall . I can do this . Lara flicked the light on and leapt to the bottom of the stairs. She swung the rifle up, planted the butt against her shoulder, and pointed the barrel toward the figure in the chair. She squeezed the trigger . The sound was a boom of thunder, filling up the entire room, and the unexpected kick of the gun nearly threw her off-balance. She didn’t waste any time; she turned and ran toward the front door . Something thudded and scraped behind her. A hand closed around her ankle, abruptly halting her movement. The rifle slipped from her hands as she fell, clattering to the floor a few feet away. Screaming, she clawed at the floorboards and kicked wildly at the thing behind her, scrabbling for the gun. The thing caught her other ankle and dragged her back. Her oversized shirt bunched around her waist, baring her . The world spun as it flipped Lara onto her back. The bot loomed over her, pressing a hand to her chest to hold her down . Its clothing was blackened and tattered. Scorched, melted skin clung to its exposed metal. It smelled faintly like burning rubber. The metal face shifted into something that might have been a human expression, if it had skin, but its teeth were locked in a skeleton grin behind the small plates that would have moved its lips.
